data.title: President Trump's Media Company Names Holding to Board to Replace McMahon
data.description:
By Sabela Ojea

Trump Media & Technology Group is adding former Congressman George Holding to its board of directors following the appointment of Linda McMahon as President Trump's secretary of education.

The company behind Truth Social on Friday said that its board voted in favor of Holding's appointment, who will take McMahon's seat.

McMahon stepped down as an independent director of the board upon her appointment as education secretary, the company said.

She was confirmed by the Senate on Monday.

Holding served as a Republican member of the U.S. Congress representing North Carolina from 2013 to 2021. Since then, he has worked as a managing director of government relations for Blackstone.

"With his extensive experience as a congressman and his wide-ranging legal background, George will bring a valued, fresh perspective to the board as TMTG strives to fulfill its plan to diversify into new industries and explore possible mergers and acquisitions," Chief Executive Devin Nunes said.

Write to Sabela Ojea at sabela.ojea@wsj.com

data.title: Dow Trims Losses to End a Tough Week — WSJ
data.description:
By Matt Wirz

An ugly week on Wall Street ended with tumultuous trading Friday as major stock indexes seesawed before finishing slightly higher. The S&P 500 index dropped 3.1% for the week, its biggest one-week decline since Sept. 6.

Investors turned defensive out of concern about the Trump administration's unpredictable tariff policies and how they would affect economic growth. That fueled sharp declines in technology and financial stocks, which had pushed indexes to record highs earlier this year.

"So much is happening from a news headline perspective," said Alex Coffey, a senior trading and derivatives strategist at Schwab. "There have been several moments in the past two weeks when we've had pretty sizable bounces in some megacap tech names, but every single time they've been getting slammed back down."

U.S. stocks fell initially on Friday when the monthly jobs report showed hiring picked up a touch in February, with 151,000 jobs added, but slightly under expectations. The unemployment rate crept up to 4.1%, while it had been forecast to hold steady.

Indexes rebounded back to positive territory after Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell delivered remarks about taking a wait-and-see approach to interest rates. Indexes still finished the week significantly lower.

Nvidia, a big beneficiary of artificial-intelligence euphoria, lost about $1 trillion of market capitalization since its stock peaked at the start of the year. Stock prices for Goldman Sachs, which recently reported a doubling of profits, sank about 17% over the same period. Tesla shares lost all of their postelection gains for the first time on Friday.

The bearish pivot pushed indexes down below thresholds that many traders use as signals to reduce risk exposure. The Dow Jones Industrial dipped below its 200-day moving average on Friday for the first time since November 2023, before rebounding and closing above the threshold.

"That puts on more pressure from automatic selling," said Keith Lerner, co-chief investment officer at Truist Advisory Services. "We're looking for more of those give-up trades and it can happen quickly."

U.S. Treasury yields edged higher, with the 10-year yield moving up to 4.317%. The greenback also weakened, with the WSJ Dollar Index dropping to its lowest level since the days after President Trump's election in November.

Bearish sentiment crept into credit markets as the yield premium, or spread, that investors demanded to own corporate bonds rather than U.S. Treasurys rose from nearly record lows in February.

"Small cracks are forming," Bank of America analyst Neha Khoda wrote in a research report Friday. "Credit has finally started to respond to volatility in equities and rates."

Stock traders are rotating out of tech and financial investments that were popular just weeks ago because they tend to outperform in periods of strong economic growth. Instead they are turning to perceived havens of safety.

European stocks, which have traded at lower multiples than their U.S. counterparts, were among the biggest gainers of the week. The German government's decision to boost borrowing for defense spending and to reboot its flagging economy prompted some U.S. investors to bargain hunt on the Continent. An iShares exchange-traded fund focused on German stocks was one of the top performers last week, gaining 6.27%.

In the U.S., McDonald's stock gained about 4.2% for the week. The fast-food restaurant chain is viewed as a strong performer in times of economic contraction when budget-conscious consumers turn away from more expensive options.

Write to Matt Wirz at matthieu.wirz@wsj.com

data.title: S&P 500 Starts March With Weekly Slide Amid Tariff Worries, Weaker-Than-Expected Jobs
data.description:
The Standard & Poor's 500 index fell 3.1% this week, pushing the market benchmark into the red for the year amid worries about tariffs and a weaker-than-expected February jobs report.

The S&P 500 ended Friday's session at 5,770.20, marking its third consecutive down week. The index has dropped 1.9% from where it ended 2024.

Investors are worried about economic impacts from the Trump administration's tariff policies as well as job cuts. Earlier this week, the administration said it would be placing 25% tariffs on Mexico and Canada, though it later delayed some of the tariffs by a month. Meanwhile, its Department of Government Efficiency has been cutting jobs across the government.

The February employment report showed nonfarm payrolls rose by 151,000, which was smaller than the increase of 160,000 jobs expected in a survey compiled by Bloomberg. The unemployment rate rose to 4.1% in February from 4% in January. No change had been expected.

All but one sector of the S&P 500 fell this week. Financials had the largest percentage drop, sliding 5.9%, followed by a 5.4% decline in consumer discretionary, a 3.8% loss in energy and a 3.4% decrease in technology.

KKR was hit hardest in the financial sector, falling 15%. The company priced 45 million shares or $2.25 billion worth of its 6.25% Series D mandatory convertible preferred stock at $50.00 per share.

Among consumer discretionary stocks, shares of Best Buy fell 12% as Chief Executive Corie Barry warned of price rises due to tariffs imposed by President Donald Trump. Best Buy also issued a full-year earnings outlook that fell short of Wall Street estimates at the midpoint.

The energy sector's drop came as crude oil futures also fell on the week. Diamondback Energy was among the decliners, losing 12% as the company said it priced a debt offering to raise $1.2 billion.

In technology, Hewlett Packard Enterprise shares shed 20% as the company issued fiscal Q2 profit guidance that trailed analysts' projections, citing industry-wide "uncertainty" from the current US tariff policy. Hewlett Packard Enterprise also shared plans to achieve roughly $350 million in run-rate cost savings by fiscal 2027 through job cuts. It plans to reduce its workforce by 5%, or about 2,500 employees, over the next 12 to 18 months.

Health care was the lone sector in positive territory for the week, edging up 0.2%.

Moderna was the sector's best performer, with its shares jumping 15% as a German court ruled that Pfizer (PFE) and its partner BioNTech (BNTX) infringed a COVID-19 vaccine patent owned by Moderna, according to a Reuters report. The court ordered Pfizer and BioNTech to disclose earnings related to the patent's use and pay a compensation to Moderna, according to the report.

Next week, earnings reports are expected from companies including Oracle (ORCL) and Adobe (ADBE).

Economic data will feature the February consumer price index and producer price index, two closely watched inflation readings.

data.title: Fairfax Financial Names Amy Sherk CFO; Jennifer Allen to Move to Chief Business Officer
data.description:
Fairfax Financial Holdings (FFH) on Friday said Amy Sherk will become the chief financial officer, while Jennifer Allen, who has held the role since 2019, will move to chief business officer, effective Monday.

Sherk, with over 20 years at Fairfax, most recently served as CFO of Fairfax India (FIH-U.TO). Debbie Chalkley, with 13 years at Fairfax, will become CFO of Fairfax India starting Monday, the company added.

"Amy has been outstanding in her numerous roles with Fairfax companies over the past 20 years and is the natural successor to Jenn, just as she was at Fairfax India. We know from our long relationship with Amy that she has the knowledge, commitment and experience to seamlessly assume the CFO position at Fairfax," Chief Executive Prem Watsa said.

data.title: US dollar sags after weaker-than-expected jobs data, Fed's Powell comments
data.description:
Key points:
  • US payrolls rise to 151,000 in February, lower than expected
  • US wage earnings growth slows
  • US rate futures price in three rate cuts in 2025
  • Euro on pace for best weekly gain in 16 years
  • Fed's Powell says cenbank in no rush to cut rates

The U.S. dollar dropped to multi-month lows against the euro and yen and fell versus most currencies on Friday after data showed the labor market in the world's largest economy slowed last month, creating fewer jobs than expected.

The report suggested that the Federal Reserve remained on track to cut interest rates multiple times this year. U.S. rate futures on Friday priced in 78 basis points (bps) of easing this year following the nonfarm payrolls report, or about three rate cuts of 25 bps each, according to LSEG calculations. The first rate reduction is likely to resume in June.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, in prepared remarks to the University of Chicago School of Business, did not really say anything new, repeating comments during his testimony before Congress and his press conference after the Fed rate decision in January.

He said on Friday the U.S. central bank will be in no rush to cut rates while it waits for more clarity on how policies of the new Trump administration affect the economy.

The euro, on the other hand, continued its winning ways, poised for its best week in 16 years with a gain of 4.5% against the dollar, boosted by Germany's game-changing fiscal reforms. It hit another four-month peak of $1.0888 after the jobs data. It last traded at $1.0845, up 0.6% .

Against the Japanese currency, the greenback was flat on the day at 147.99 yen , after earlier falling to a five-month low of 146.94 yen.

The dollar extended its losses after data showed nonfarm payrolls increased by 151,000 jobs last month after rising by a downwardly revised 125,000 in January. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast payrolls gaining 160,000 jobs after a previously reported 143,000 rise in January.

The dollar index , which measures the greenback's value versus six major currencies, has fallen 3.5% this week, on track for its worst weekly performance since November 2022. It fell 0.4% on Friday to 103.81, after earlier sliding to its lowest since early November.

Natalia Lojevsky, managing director, at CIFC Asset Management, with $44 billion in assets under management thinks "the softer average hourly earnings is probably a relief for the Fed, who continue to evaluate inflationary pressures both in the labor market and broader economy as a result of the yet uncertain trade and tariff policy."

Last month's average hourly earnings, a measure of wage inflation, moderated to a 0.3% rise, from an 0.5% increase in January.

Overall, it has been a volatile week for the currency market, driven mainly by U.S. trade and economic growth uncertainties and a pivotal development in Europe as its largest economy abandoned its fiscal constraints to boost spending and revive growth.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said on Friday the U.S. economy may slow as it transitions away from public spending towards more private spending, calling it a "detox period" needed to reach a more sustainable equilibrium.

Another reprieve of levies aimed at Mexico and Canada announced by President Donald Trump on Thursday offered little relief to whiplashed markets. The exemption expires on April 2 when Trump said he will impose reciprocal tariffs on all U.S. trading partners.

On Friday, Fed Chair Powell said a one-time jump in prices due to tariffs does not need a monetary policy response .

"The Fed is going to be very conservative on what they say until we know whether or not the tariffs are tactical or strategic, and if they do turn out to be strategic, they're still going to wait until the hard data demonstrates both that inflation is low, and the economy is in recession before they really do very much," said Tony Roth, chief investment officer at Wilmington Trust Investment Advisors.

The greenback rose 0.4% against the Canadian dollar to C$1.4362, but slipped versus the Mexican peso to 20.259 pesos.

In cryptocurrencies, both bitcoin and ether fell in the midst of a summit of industry leaders at the White House on Friday, which is likely to focus on Trump's plans to build a strategic reserve containing bitcoin and four other coins.

Trump on Thursday signed an executive order to establish the strategic bitcoin reserve , directing the secretaries of Treasury and Commerce to develop "budget-neutral strategies" for acquiring additional bitcoin that have no "incremental costs" on taxpayers.

Bitcoin was last down 2.8% at $87,030.54 , while ether last changed hands at $2,150 , down 2.6%.

data.title: Wall St ends higher after Fed chief's comments, but posts big weekly loss
data.description:
Key points:
  • Hewlett Packard slumps after dour second-quarter forecasts
  • Costco falls after retailer misses estimates
  • Nonfarm payrolls increase by 151,000 in February

U.S. stocks finished higher on Friday, rebounding from early declines after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said the economy was "in a good place," but uncertainty about U.S. trade policy led to Wall Street's biggest weekly decline in months.

Powell said the central bank will not be quick to cut interest rates and echoed concerns about President Donald Trump's policies.

Markets have been roiled this week by uncertainty about Trump's tariff decisions on imported goods from Canada, Mexico and China.

The benchmark S&P 500 finished with its biggest weekly loss since September. The S&P 500 and the Nasdaq also registered their third straight week of declines, the longest losing streak since mid-July and early August last year.

Powell said the Fed will take a cautious approach to monetary policy easing, adding the economy currently "continues to be in a good place".

"Powell is echoing what the rest of us feel: unease that while the adjustments made by the administration may well work and put the country on better financial footing, the speed and whipsaw-like nature of the change makes it difficult to predict and to plan around," said Jamie Cox, managing partner at Harris Financial Group in Richmond, Virginia. "So, the best action when that occurs is to sit and wait."

Stocks fell in choppy early trade, but rebounded after Powell's comments. The three main indexes ended the week lower, with In the previous session, the Nasdaq confirmed a 10% drop from its December all-time high.

Utilities , energy , technology and industrials were the biggest gainers among the S&P 500's 11 main sectors. Consumer discretionary , financials and consumer staples were the biggest drag.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 222.64 points, or 0.52%, to 42,801.72, the S&P 500 gained 31.68 points, or 0.55%, to 5,770.20 and the Nasdaq Composite gained 126.97 points, or 0.70%, to 18,196.22.

For the week, the S&P 500 ended down 3.1%, the Nasdaq declined 3.45%, and the Dow fell 2.37%. The Russell 2000 Small Cap index fell 3.86%.

Data early on Friday showed U.S. job growth picked up in February from the previous month. However, thousands of recent firings of federal workers were not reflected in the data.

Unemployment ticked up to 4.1%, adding to worries about the economy's resilience. Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs have lowered their growth forecasts for the economy.

"This is a growth scare," said Adam Hetts, portfolio manager at Janus Henderson Investors. "This is what it feels like to go from a no-landing to a soft-landing environment and it's unpleasant. It involves a spate of unpleasant economic data, and the primary driver being weaker consumer spending."

On Thursday, Trump offered a four-week reprieve on tariffs he imposed on imports from Canada and Mexico that fall under a free-trade pact. The U.S. remains in a trade war with China.

Reciprocal trade barriers and other duties are expected to take effect in the following weeks.

Hewlett Packard Enterprise slumped 12% after saying its annual profit forecast would be hit by U.S. tariffs.

Costco fell 6% after the retailer missed Wall Street estimates on quarterly earnings as merchandise costs increased.

Broadcom gained 8.6% after the chipmaker assuaged investor worries about artificial intelligence infrastructure demand with a strong second-quarter forecast.

Advancing issues outnumbered decliners by a 1.35-to-1 ratio on the NYSE. There were 92 new highs and 136 new lows on the NYSE.

The S&P 500 posted 8 new 52-week highs and 13 new lows while the Nasdaq Composite recorded 28 new highs and 159 new lows.

About 16.92 billion worth of shares were traded across U.S. exchanges, compared with the 20-day moving average of 16.23 billion shares.

data.title: Unity Biotechnology, Inc. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Unity Biotechnology, Inc., a biotechnology firm focused on developing therapeutics to address diseases of aging, has released its 2024 Form 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces as it advances its clinical programs, particularly in ophthalmology.

Financial Highlights

  • Licensing Revenue – Related Party: $0 million. The company did not generate any licensing revenue for the year ended December 31, 2024.
  • Total Operating Expenses: $31.171 million, a decrease from $44.667 million in 2023, primarily due to reductions in research and development and general and administrative expenses.
  • Loss from Operations: $(31.171) million, improved from a loss of $(44.667) million in 2023, reflecting cost-saving measures.
  • Net Loss: $(25.990) million, a reduction from $(39.860) million in 2023, attributed to decreased operating expenses and improved financial management.
  • Net Loss Per Share, Basic and Diluted: $(1.54), compared to $(2.70) in 2023, indicating a reduction in net loss per share due to cost management and restructuring efforts.

Business Highlights

  • Business Overview: Unity Biotechnology is focused on developing therapeutics to address diseases of aging, with a primary emphasis on senolytic medicines that target senescent cells to treat age-related diseases such as ophthalmologic conditions.
  • Ophthalmology Program: UBX1325 is the lead drug candidate for treating age-related eye diseases, including diabetic macular edema (DME) and neovascular age-related macular degeneration (nAMD). The company has conducted multiple clinical trials demonstrating the drug's safety and efficacy.
  • Clinical Trials and Data: The Phase 2 BEHOLD study showed that a single injection of UBX1325 led to significant improvements in visual acuity and retinal structure in patients with DME. The ongoing Phase 2b ASPIRE study is comparing UBX1325 to aflibercept in patients with DME.
  • Pipeline and Development Focus: The company has paused other pipeline programs to focus resources on the advanced development of UBX1325. Additional programs targeting Tie2 signaling and α-Klotho hormone have been outlicensed or paused.
  • Manufacturing and Supply Chain: Unity Biotechnology relies on third-party manufacturers for clinical drug supply and plans to establish long-term supply agreements to ensure reliable, high-quality production as it approaches commercial operations.
  • Regulatory Interactions: The company has engaged with the FDA to discuss the design of pivotal trials for UBX1325, with expectations for a non-inferiority trial comparing it to an approved anti-VEGF agent.
  • Intellectual Property: Unity Biotechnology holds a robust patent portfolio, including patents related to its cellular senescence program and UBX1325, with exclusivity expected to extend to 2039 in the U.S.
  • Future Outlook: The company anticipates receiving topline data from the ASPIRE study in 2025 and is focused on advancing UBX1325 through clinical development to potentially transform treatment paradigms for age-related eye diseases.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Focus: Unity Biotechnology has focused on advancing its clinical programs, particularly the development of UBX1325, through various phases of clinical trials. The company has also engaged in restructuring efforts to align resources with its strategic focus on ophthalmology programs, which included a reduction in workforce to optimize resource allocation and extend its financial runway.
  • Capital Management: The company has engaged in several capital management activities, including the issuance of common stock and warrants through a Follow-On Offering and an Inducement Offer, raising approximately $45 million and $4.4 million, respectively. Additionally, Unity Biotechnology entered into a Loan and Security Agreement with Hercules Capital, which was later terminated after a voluntary prepayment. The company also executed a 1-for-10 reverse stock split to adjust its share structure.
  • Future Outlook: Unity Biotechnology anticipates the need to raise additional capital to finance its operations beyond the fourth quarter of 2025. The company plans to continue seeking opportunities for financing through equity offerings, collaborations, or other means to support its ongoing clinical development and potential commercialization efforts. The company acknowledges the substantial doubt about its ability to continue as a going concern and is actively exploring strategic options to secure necessary funding.

Challenges and Risks

  • Limited Operating History: The company faces significant risks due to its limited operating history as a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company with no products approved for commercial sale. This makes it difficult to assess future viability and increases the uncertainty of achieving profitability.
  • Financial Condition: The company's financial condition raises substantial doubt about its ability to continue as a going concern. Existing capital resources are expected to fund operations only into the fourth quarter of 2025, necessitating additional funding to continue operations and development activities. Failure to secure additional financing could adversely impact the company's ability to achieve business objectives and may lead to liquidation of assets.
  • Reliance on UBX1325: The company is heavily reliant on the successful development of UBX1325, which is in Phase 2 of clinical development. Any failure in its development could significantly impact the company's financial condition and future prospects, as it has limited resources to develop additional drug candidates.
  • Operational Risks: The company faces operational risks related to its dependence on third-party suppliers and manufacturers for the production of its drug candidates. Any disruption in the supply chain could delay clinical trials and commercialization efforts.
  • Regulatory Risks: Regulatory risks are significant, as the company must navigate complex approval processes for its novel senolytic medicines. The FDA and other regulatory bodies have limited experience with senescence, which may increase the complexity and length of the approval process.
  • Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks, including fluctuations in financial markets that could impact its ability to raise capital. Economic conditions such as high interest rates, rising inflation, and potential recessions may further exacerbate these risks. Additionally, the company faces risks related to compliance with Nasdaq listing requirements, which could affect its stock price and ability to raise capital.

SEC Filing: Unity Biotechnology, Inc. [ UBX ] - 10-K - Mar. 07, 2025

data.title: NOODLES & Co SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Noodles & Company, a fast-casual restaurant chain known for its diverse noodle dishes, has released its 2024 Form 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the current market environment.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenue: $493.3 million, a decrease of 2.0% from 2023, primarily due to a decline in company same store sales, refranchising, and permanent restaurant closures, partially offset by growth in new restaurant revenue.
  • Loss from Operations: $(27.8) million, increased from a loss of $(5.0) million in 2023, primarily due to increased restaurant impairments, closure costs, and asset disposals.
  • Net Loss: $(36.2) million, increased from a net loss of $(9.9) million in 2023, driven by higher interest expense and increased impairment charges.
  • Loss per Class A and Class B common stock, combined: $(0.80), compared to $(0.21) in 2023, reflecting the increased net loss.
  • Cost of Sales: $123.7 million, decreased by 0.3% from 2023, but as a percentage of restaurant revenue, increased to 25.6% from 25.2% due to menu mix shifts and inflation.

Business Highlights

  • Restaurant Portfolio: As of December 31, 2024, Noodles & Company operated 463 restaurants across 31 states, comprising 371 company-owned and 92 franchise locations. The company continues to analyze its restaurant prototype design to facilitate future expansion and meet changing consumer needs.
  • Digital Sales Growth: In 2024, approximately 56% of sales were derived from digital ordering, highlighting the company's focus on convenience and accessibility through its mobile app, website, and third-party delivery services.
  • Menu Innovation: In late 2024, Noodles & Company initiated a comprehensive menu upgrade, introducing reimagined favorites with more sauce, vegetables, and premium ingredients, alongside new dishes with bold flavors. This rollout is expected to be substantially complete by mid-2025.
  • Restaurant Development: In 2024, the company opened ten new company-owned restaurants and plans to open two more in 2025. The company also closed thirteen underperforming locations in 2024.
  • Franchising Strategy: The company sold six company-owned restaurants to a franchisee in 2024 and entered into a six-year development plan to open ten new locations in Oregon and Washington. Franchising remains a key growth strategy, with 10 area developers committed to opening 119 restaurants.
  • Training and Career Development: Noodles & Company emphasizes continuous learning and professional growth, with designated team members leading training efforts to maintain a consistent approach to development.
  • Food Preparation and Quality: The company focuses on high-quality food preparation using classic cooking methods and maintains strict food safety and quality assurance programs.
  • Human Capital Management: The company prioritizes diversity and inclusion, offering competitive wages, benefits, and career development opportunities. The Noodles & Company Foundation supports team members with emergency assistance and scholarships.
  • Site Development and Expansion: The company uses a combination of internal teams and external consultants for site selection, focusing on key site characteristics to drive successful locations. Due to increased costs and lower returns, the company has reduced its new restaurant development pipeline for 2025 and 2026.
  • Operational Challenges: The company faced a 1.5% decrease in system-wide comparable restaurant sales in 2024, with a focus on revitalizing menu options to address sales declines.
  • Cost Management: The company experienced less than 2% commodity inflation in 2024 and managed to partially mitigate wage inflation through labor efficiencies.
  • Future Outlook: Noodles & Company plans to continue enhancing its menu, expanding its digital capabilities, and leveraging its franchising strategy to drive growth and adapt to consumer trends.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Initiatives: Noodles & Company has focused on strategic initiatives to enhance its financial flexibility and operational efficiency. The company has been actively managing its restaurant portfolio by closing underperforming locations and refranchising company-owned restaurants to franchisees. In 2024, the company sold six company-owned restaurants to a franchisee, which is part of its strategy to optimize its restaurant base and improve profitability. Additionally, the company is investing in technology improvements and digital menu boards to enhance customer experience and operational efficiency.
  • Capital Management: The company has been actively managing its capital structure through various initiatives. In 2024, Noodles & Company amended its Amended and Restated Credit Agreement to increase the credit facility to $125 million and adjust interest rate ranges, providing greater financial flexibility. The company also repurchased 1,731,952 shares of its common stock for approximately $5 million under the 2023 Share Repurchase Program, which was subsequently canceled. The company maintains a revolving credit facility with $103 million of indebtedness as of December 31, 2024, and has been in compliance with all debt covenants.
  • Future Outlook: Looking ahead, Noodles & Company plans to continue optimizing its restaurant portfolio by closing underperforming locations and focusing on new restaurant openings in strategic locations. The company expects to open two new company-owned restaurants in 2025 and continue investing in technology improvements. Capital expenditures for 2025 are expected to be in the range of $11 million to $13 million, primarily for reinvestment in existing restaurants and technology enhancements. The company aims to maintain sufficient liquidity through cash flows from operations and its revolving credit facility to support its strategic initiatives and capital management activities.

Challenges and Risks

  • Economic Conditions: Changes in economic conditions, such as higher inflationary pressures and elevated interest rates, may reduce customer demand and increase operational costs. The company has experienced increased borrowing rates and food ingredient costs, particularly for chicken, which led to temporary price surcharges.
  • Competition: The restaurant industry is highly competitive, with Noodles & Company facing competition from casual dining, fast-casual, and quick-service segments. Competitors may have more resources and offer lower-cost options, impacting the company's market share.
  • Marketing and Brand Risks: The effectiveness of marketing programs is crucial, and failure to compete with better-resourced competitors could materially affect financial performance. Negative publicity, especially through social media, could damage the brand and reduce sales.
  • Food Safety: The risk of foodborne illnesses remains a concern, particularly with the expansion of catering and delivery services, which are harder to control.
  • Labor and Franchise Risks: The company faces challenges in hiring and retaining qualified employees, which could increase labor costs and impact operations. Franchisees may not operate consistently with company standards, affecting the brand and financial results.
  • Technology and Cybersecurity: Reliance on information technology systems poses risks of security breaches, which could harm the company's reputation and financial condition.
  • Supply Chain: Disruptions in the supply chain, including reliance on certain vendors and suppliers, could adversely affect operations and financial results.
  • Regulatory Risks: Compliance with various federal, state, and local regulations, including labor laws and health standards, is necessary. Changes in these regulations could increase operational costs.
  • Management Strategies: Management is addressing these challenges by focusing on menu innovation, enhancing digital platforms, and optimizing restaurant operations. The company is also exploring new restaurant prototypes to address cost concerns and changing consumer behaviors. Strategic investments in digital capabilities and marketing are aimed at increasing brand engagement and loyalty.
  • Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks, including fluctuations in commodity prices and interest rates. The recent amendments to the credit agreement have resulted in increased borrowing rates, which could impact financial performance. The company is also vulnerable to changes in consumer preferences and economic conditions, which may affect sales and profitability.

data.title: FIRST NORTHERN COMMUNITY BANCORP SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
First Northern Community Bancorp, a bank holding company providing community banking services through its subsidiary, First Northern Bank of Dixon, has released its 2024 Form 10-K report. The report details the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the current economic and regulatory environment.

Financial Highlights

  • Net Income: $20.0 million, a 7.1% decrease compared to $21.6 million for 2023.
  • Net Income Per Common Share: $1.26, a decrease of 6.7% compared to $1.35 for 2023.
  • Net Income Per Common Share on a Fully Diluted Basis: $1.24, a decrease of 7.5% compared to $1.34 for 2023.
  • Net Interest Income: $64.4 million, a decrease of 3.3% from $66.5 million in 2023, primarily due to an increase in interest expense.
  • Net Interest Margin: 3.60% for 2024, a 2.6% or 10 basis point decrease from 3.70% for 2023.
  • Non-Interest Income: $6.0 million, a decrease of 23.3% from $7.8 million in 2023, primarily due to a gain on bargain purchase in 2023 not repeated in 2024.
  • Non-Interest Expenses: $42.8 million, down 2.0% from $43.6 million in 2023, primarily due to decreases in salaries and employee benefits.

Business Highlights

  • Company Overview: First Northern Community Bancorp is a bank holding company providing community banking services through its subsidiary, First Northern Bank of Dixon, across several California counties. The company focuses on serving individuals and small- to medium-sized businesses.
  • Branch Network Expansion: In January 2023, the company expanded its footprint by acquiring three branches from Columbia State Bank in Colusa, Orland, and Willows, California, enhancing its presence in these regions.
  • Service Offerings: The bank offers a wide range of products and services, including commercial, consumer, and real estate loans, as well as non-FDIC insured investment and brokerage services through Raymond James Financial Services.
  • Revenue Segments: The bank generates most of its revenue from interest income, primarily from loans and leases, investments, and interest-bearing accounts. Interest income from loans and leases accounted for approximately 70% of the total interest income in 2024.
  • Geographical Performance: The bank's deposits are primarily sourced from northern and central Solano County, southern and central Yolo County, and Placer County, with no significant concentration in a single industry or depositor group.
  • Operational Highlights: The bank operates fourteen full-service branches and additional offices for mortgage loans and investment services, with a focus on supporting small- and medium-sized businesses and individual customers.
  • Regulatory Environment: The company is subject to extensive regulation, including the Bank Holding Company Act, and must comply with various federal and state banking regulations, which can impact its operations and financial condition.
  • Future Outlook: The company continues to monitor regulatory changes and economic conditions that could affect its business operations, with a focus on maintaining compliance and adapting to evolving market demands.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Initiatives: The company has focused on maintaining a strong capital position to support growth and profitability. This includes a stock repurchase program initiated on March 27, 2024, allowing for repurchases of up to 6% of the company's outstanding shares, aimed at managing capital and creating liquidity for shareholders. Additionally, the company has been actively managing its investment portfolio, increasing investments by $61.5 million in 2024 to enhance returns and liquidity.
  • Capital Management: The company reported an increase in stockholders' equity to $176.3 million as of December 31, 2024, primarily due to net income of $20.0 million. The company also repurchased 389,071 shares of its common stock during 2024, with 590,624 shares remaining available for repurchase under the current program. The company has not paid cash dividends recently, opting instead for stock dividends, with a 5% stock dividend declared for March 25, 2025. The company maintains a strong liquidity position with core deposits representing 87% of total assets and a net loan-to-deposit ratio of 61.6%.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to continue its stock repurchase program through April 30, 2026, and maintain its focus on capital management to support future growth. The company does not anticipate paying cash dividends in the near future, preferring to reinvest earnings to strengthen its capital base. The company will also continue to manage its investment portfolio to optimize returns and maintain liquidity, while exploring opportunities for strategic growth and expansion.

Challenges and Risks

  • Market Volatility: Recent developments in the banking industry, including high-profile bank failures, have led to increased market volatility and customer concerns about the safety of regional banks. This could impact the company's liquidity and funding costs.
  • Regulatory Changes: Potential legislative and regulatory changes could impose higher capital requirements and increase deposit insurance assessments, affecting profitability.
  • Economic Conditions: Economic conditions in the U.S. may soften or become recessionary, impacting the financial services industry and the bank's operations. The Federal Reserve's monetary policy tightening and geopolitical instability increase recession risks, potentially affecting loan demand and asset quality.
  • Commercial and Real Estate Lending Risks: The bank's focus on commercial and real estate lending exposes it to risks associated with economic downturns, natural disasters, and industry-specific challenges, particularly in California. Recent wildfires and insurance market contractions in California could increase the risk of loss in the bank's loan portfolio.
  • Interest Rate Risk: The bank is subject to interest rate risk, with changes in interest rates potentially affecting net interest margins and loan demand. Inflationary pressures and geopolitical events, such as the Russia-Ukraine conflict, could exacerbate these risks.
  • Regulatory Risks: Regulatory risks include potential changes in banking laws and increased scrutiny on climate-related risks. The bank may face increased compliance costs and operational challenges as regulatory expectations evolve.
  • Government Policies: The company's operations are influenced by government monetary and fiscal policies, which affect interest rates and the availability of loans and deposits. Changes in these policies could impact the bank's growth and earnings.
  • Competitive Pressures: Management is aware of the competitive pressures from larger banks and non-traditional financial services firms, which could impact the bank's market share and profitability. The company is focused on maintaining adequate capital levels and managing interest rate risks to mitigate potential impacts on financial performance.
  • Market Risks: The bank is exposed to market risks, including interest rate fluctuations and changes in economic conditions, which could affect its financial results. Management is monitoring these risks and implementing strategies to manage interest rate exposure and maintain financial stability.

data.title: Blackstone Real Estate Income Trust, Inc. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Blackstone Real Estate Income Trust, Inc. (BREIT), a leading real estate investment trust focused on income-generating commercial real estate, has released its 2024 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges and risks it faces.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenues: $8,531 million, reflecting a decrease of $403 million compared to the previous year, primarily due to real estate dispositions.
  • Net Loss: $979.8 million, a slight improvement of $0.2 million from the previous year.
  • Net Loss Per Share of Common Stock — Basic and Diluted: $(0.23), a decrease of $0.07 compared to the previous year.
  • Same Property NOI Attributable to BREIT Stockholders: $4,836.9 million, an increase of $190.4 million or 4% from the previous year, driven by higher base rental revenue and tenant reimbursement income.

Business Highlights

  • Business Segments: BREIT operates in nine reportable segments: Rental Housing, Industrial, Net Lease, Data Centers, Hospitality, Self Storage, Retail, Office properties, and Investments in Real Estate Debt. Rental Housing includes multifamily, student, affordable, manufactured, and single-family rental housing, as well as senior living.
  • Geographical Performance: BREIT's real estate portfolio is primarily concentrated in the South (38%), West (29%), and East (20%) regions of the United States. The company also has investments in Europe and Canada.
  • Sales Units: As of December 31, 2024, BREIT owned a diversified portfolio of income-producing assets comprising 4,569 properties and 62,907 single-family rental homes.
  • Investment Strategy: BREIT focuses on acquiring stabilized, income-generating commercial real estate across various asset classes in the United States and, to a lesser extent, outside the United States. The strategy leverages Blackstone's scale and real-time data insights to identify and acquire investments at attractive pricing.
  • Real Estate Debt Investments: BREIT's real estate debt investments focus on non-distressed public and private real estate debt, including CMBS, RMBS, and other real estate-related corporate credit. These investments are primarily in the United States but also include international exposure.
  • Operational Highlights: In 2024, BREIT sold 128 rental housing properties, 114 industrial properties, 15 retail properties, three hospitality properties, and one self-storage property, generating total net proceeds of $9.0 billion.
  • Joint Ventures: BREIT formed a joint venture with another Blackstone-advised investment vehicle to acquire all outstanding common shares of Tricon Residential Inc., maintaining an 11.6% ownership stake.
  • Future Outlook: BREIT intends to continue selling shares of its common stock on a monthly basis through continuous public and private offerings, aiming to further expand its investment portfolio and enhance shareholder value.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Real Estate Dispositions: The company has focused on strategic real estate dispositions, selling 128 rental housing properties, 114 industrial properties, and other assets for net proceeds of $9.0 billion, recognizing a net realized gain of $1.7 billion.
  • Joint Venture Formation: A joint venture was formed to acquire Tricon Residential Inc., converting prior investments into a new joint venture interest.
  • Capital Management: The company raised $3.4 billion from the sale of shares and units, while repurchasing $9.4 billion of shares and units from investors. It repaid a net $2.5 billion of financings and maintained a leverage ratio of 49%. The company has $6.2 billion in liquidity, including $4.5 billion in undrawn revolving credit facilities and $1.7 billion in cash. The company also registered an offering of up to $60 billion in shares, receiving $16.6 billion in net proceeds from the current offering. The company fulfilled all repurchase requests for the year, totaling $9.4 billion.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to continue selling shares of its common stock on a monthly basis through continuous public and private offerings. It aims to maintain its liquidity position to support business operations and meet capital obligations, including potential distributions, operating expenses, and debt service. The company remains focused on strategic real estate investments and dispositions to optimize its portfolio and enhance shareholder value.

Challenges and Risks

  • Organizational Structure Risks: The company faces significant risks related to its organizational structure, including the speculative nature of its investments due to limited historical data on current holdings and the inability of investors to evaluate future investments. The broad discretion granted to the Adviser in selecting investments may lead to riskier decisions that could adversely affect financial results. Additionally, the lack of a public trading market for the company's common stock limits liquidity options for investors, potentially resulting in sales at a loss.
  • Financial Challenges: The company has incurred substantial net losses in recent years, primarily due to real estate depreciation and amortization expenses under GAAP, and may continue to face financial challenges. The use of non-GAAP financial measures like FFO, AFFO, and FAD, which are not directly comparable to GAAP net income, adds complexity to financial evaluations.
  • Operational Risks: Operational risks include the potential for costly and irreversible transactions made by the Adviser without board approval, and the limited ability to repurchase shares due to liquidity constraints and repurchase plan limitations. Economic events, such as market volatility and geopolitical tensions, could exacerbate these liquidity challenges, impacting cash flow and financial stability.
  • Capital Deployment Risks: The company also faces risks related to the deployment of capital, as delays in identifying suitable investments could lead to holding significant cash reserves, diluting investment returns. The integration of new investments and management of growth pose additional operational challenges that could strain resources and affect financial performance.
  • Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks, including interest rate fluctuations and inflation, which could adversely affect financial conditions and results of operations. The ongoing geopolitical tensions and economic uncertainties, such as those arising from trade conflicts and public health emergencies, further contribute to market volatility and potential adverse impacts on the company's performance.

data.title: Equity Markets Post Weekly Declines Amid Tariff Uncertainty
data.description:
US benchmark equity indexes closed higher Friday, but posted sharp declines for the week amid uncertainty regarding the Trump administration's trade policies.

The Nasdaq Composite rose 0.7% to 18,196.2 Friday, while the S&P 500 gained 0.6% to 5,770.2. The Dow Jones Industrial Average advanced 0.5% to 42,801.7. Among sectors, utilities led the gainers, while consumer staples saw the biggest drop.

The Nasdaq slid 3.5% this week, while the S&P 500 was down 3.1%. The Dow fell 2.4%.

"Shifting tariffs on Mexico, Canada, and China, alongside threats to extend the same to the (European Union), are sowing uncertainty and instability, triggering market turbulence," Nigel Green, chief executive of financial advisory and asset management firm deVere Group, said Friday.

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said that the central bank would want "greater clarity" around the Trump administration's policy changes before making its next move on interest rates.

"As we parse the incoming information (about policy changes), we are focused on separating the signal from the noise as the outlook evolves," Powell said. "Despite elevated levels of uncertainty, the US economy continues to be in a good place."

The White House could impose tariffs on Canadian lumber and dairy products as early as Friday to match Canada's current tariffs on US goods, Bloomberg News reported, citing Trump. On Thursday, the US administration issued temporary tariff exemptions for Canadian and Mexican goods that comply with a North American trade pact. The US recently doubled its levy on Chinese imports. Canada and China have announced retaliatory measures.

US Treasury yields rose Friday, with the 10-year rate gaining 2.5 basis points to 4.31% and the two-year rate adding 3.9 basis points to 4%.

In economic news, nonfarm payrolls in the US rose by 151,000 last month, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported. The consensus was for a 160,000 increase, according to a survey compiled by Bloomberg. The unemployment rate increased to 4.1% from January's 4%, which was the market view for February.

"Job growth is likely to soften over the coming months, as federal layoffs related to (the Department of Government Efficiency) continue to mount and ongoing trade policy uncertainty helps to weigh on near-term hiring intentions," TD Economics said.

West Texas Intermediate crude oil increased 1% to $67.04 a barrel Friday.

In company news, Broadcom's shares jumped 8.6%, the best performer on the S&P 500. The chipmaker late Thursday provided an upbeat outlook for its fiscal second-quarter revenue after delivering a first-quarter beat.

Walgreens Boots Alliance was among the top S&P 500 gainers Friday, up 7.5%. Late Thursday, the drugstore chain agreed to be acquired and taken private by an affiliate of Sycamore Partners in a deal worth up to $23.7 billion.

Hewlett Packard Enterprise's shares plunged 12% Friday, the worst performer on the S&P 500. The company late Thursday issued a downbeat full-year earnings outlook and announced plans to cut jobs.

Costco Wholesale declined 6.1% Friday. Late Thursday, the warehouse chain posted a quarterly earnings miss.

Gold fell 0.3% to $2,918.90 per troy ounce, while silver slid 1.2% to $32.95 per ounce.

data.title: CBOT wheat futures close lower in subdued trade
data.description:
Chicago Board of Trade wheat futures closed lower on Friday on improving French winter wheat conditions and a lack of supportive news, traders said.

  • Traders shrugged off weakness in the dollar , which is normally a supportive factor for grains by making U.S. goods more competitive globally.

  • CBOT May soft red winter wheat (WK25) settled down 2-3/4 cents at $5.51-1/4 per bushel. For the week, the May contract ended down 4-1/2 cents a bushel or 0.8%.

  • K.C. May hard red winter wheat (KWK25) finished down 1 cent on Friday at $5.64-3/4 a bushel and Minneapolis May spring wheat (MWEK25) settled down 1-1/4 cents at $5.92-3/4.

  • Ratings for France's main wheat crop improved slightly in the last week but remained close to last year's rain-hit levels, farm office FranceAgriMer reported.

  • European wheat futures fell to a six-month low.

  • Traders are also monitoring dryness in the U.S. Plains winter wheat belt. Some 24% of the winter wheat crop nationally was in an area experiencing drought as of March 4, the U.S. Department of Agriculture said on Thursday, up from 22% the previous week.

  • Market players await USDA's next monthly supply/demand report on March 11.

data.title: Atara Biotherapeutics to Cut Half of Workforce Amid Restructuring
data.description:
By Connor Hart

Atara Biotherapeutics said it would lay off half of its workforce in a restructuring that came as the company narrowed its loss and logged higher revenue in the fourth quarter.

The immunotherapy company said Friday that it will retain about 35 employees, whose roles are essential to operations.

The company had 225 employees as of Dec. 31, 2023, according to the latest headcount available in filings with the Securities and Exchange Commission. However, other workforce reductions have taken place since then, including in January 2024, when the company's workforce was cut by a quarter.

Chief Executive Cokey Nguyen said the restructuring will allow the company to further narrow its focus on Ebvallo, a treatment candidate for Epstein-Barr virus positive post-transplant lymphoproliferative disease, or EBV+PTLD.

The development of Ebvallo was halted earlier this year after the Food and Drug Administration placed the drug on a clinical hold. Nguyen said that the company will continue to work toward a path to release the clinical hold and resubmit an application for the treatment.

At the same time, the company will pause development of its allogeneic CAR-T cell programs and discontinue all CAR-T operations, he said.

For the fourth quarter, Atara posted a net loss of $12.7 million, compared with a loss of $60.5 million a year earlier.

On a per-share basis, the company reported a quarterly loss of $1.19. Analysts surveyed by FactSet expected a loss of $2.86 a share.

Revenue jumped to $32.8 million from $4.3 million a year earlier and came in ahead of the $20.6 million that analysts modeled.

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com

data.title: UK bans import of cattle, pigs sheep and deer from Hungary, Slovakia
data.description: Britain introduced an import ban on cattle, pigs, sheep and deer from Hungary and Slovakia to prevent the spread of Foot and Mouth disease (FMD) following a confirmed case in Hungary, the government said in a statement on Friday.

data.title: Beyond Oil Secures Global Supplier Approval from Restaurant Brands International
data.description:
Beyond Oil (BOIL.CN) after trade Friday said it received global supplier approval from Restaurant Brands International , one of the world's largest quick-service restaurant companies.

With this approval, Beyond Oi, which offers frying-oil solutions, said it is now authorized to begin accessing RBI brands, which include Burger King (19, 700 locations), Popeye's (4, 900 locations), Tim Horton's (6, 000 locations)

and Firehouse Subs (400 locations).

"This milestone enables Beyond Oil to collaborate with RBI franchisees worldwide, providing them with its proprietary oil filtration and management solutions that extend frying oil life, enhance food quality, and support sustainability initiatives," the company said.

It added: "The approval marks a significant step in Beyond Oil's strategy to expand its presence across the global foodservice sector."

The company's shares closed down $0.08 to $2.77 on the Canadian Securities Exchange.

data.title: As US restores some aid, humanitarian groups ask: where is the money?
data.description:
Key points:
  • UN says 'severe funding cuts have been a body blow to our work'
  • Two global aid groups had some terminated programs reinstated
  • After saying aid review done, US sent out survey to aid groups

Days after cutting billions of dollars in foreign aid programs, the United States began reversing some decisions, but humanitarian groups say funds have not yet arrived and the United Nations has started evaluating "choices we are making on which lives not to save" due to cuts.

U.S. President Donald Trump returned to office on January 20 and immediately ordered a 90-day pause on foreign aid pending a review of whether the programs align with his "America First" foreign policy.

The review, which had granted waivers for life-saving and emergency food aid, has been marked by what aid officials describe as chaos and confusion.

"Severe funding cuts have been a body blow to our work to save lives," U.N. aid chief Tom Fletcher said on Thursday. "It is the pace at which so much vital work has been shut down that adds to the perfect storm that we face."

Fletcher said he has asked U.N. humanitarian coordinators around the world to report by the end of next week "on where we will need to cut back most dramatically, and the implications of the tough choices we are making on which lives not to save."

The U.S. has been the world's biggest aid donor, but - less than halfway through the 90-day review - the State Department said last week that it had completed its assessment and terminated 92% of USAID foreign aid contracts, worth some $54 billion.

Some of the terminations were quickly reversed. Then, despite the State Department having said the review was complete, aid groups this week were asked to fill out a U.S. government survey asking whether projects "conform to the Trump Administration's goal of aligning foreign assistance with American interests and values."

State Department spokesperson Tammy Bruce said on Thursday that if programs had been cut it was because "they did not serve either American interests or make us safer, more secure, or more prosperous," adding: "Those decisions are going to be made – clearly, some have been – in the process of the review."

'PARALYZED'

Last week the U.N. Population Fund said the U.S. had cut 48 grants worth about $377 million for critical maternal health care, protection from violence, rape treatment and other life-saving care in more than 25 countries. On Tuesday, Washington reversed its decision on seven of those programs.

The Norwegian Refugee Council said it has 29 U.S.-funded projects and received 24 termination notices, many for programs that had been operating under waivers during the review. This week, the U.S. reinstated 10 of the terminated projects.

"Our many lifesaving U.S.-funded projects that have gone from suspension to waivers to termination to revision and now green light will still be paralyzed as there still has been no reimbursement of costs incurred and no clarity on when we will be reimbursed," NRC Secretary General Jan Egeland told Reuters.

"So we have no liquidity to resume lifesaving aid and war victims, most of them women and children, are left with no relief," said Egeland, a former U.N. aid chief, adding NRC was owed tens of millions of dollars for work done.

On March 5, the U.S. Supreme Court said the Trump administration could not withhold payments to foreign aid groups for already completed work.

The International Rescue Committee said it is also owed tens of millions of dollars for completed work.

Last week IRC received termination notices for 42 of its 77 programs. Among those terminated were 17 programs that had received life-saving aid waivers. Just a few days later the U.S. reversed its decision and reinstated 12 programs - 11 of which were among those considered life-saving according to the waiver.

"We still have five grants that had life-saving activities that had previously been waived, that are counted in the termination notices," said Ciaran Donnelly, head of IRC's crisis response, recovery and development. "These aren't things where you can't just turn them on and off at the drop of a hat."

He said some of the terminated projects covered activities in countries such as South Sudan, Yemen and Bangladesh on malnutrition, safe water and essential health care projects, including specialized services for women and girls.

"We're determined to do everything we can, to deliver every last ounce of humanitarian assistance that we can. So getting clarity on expectations and confidence on funding guarantees to be able to do that is critical," he said.

"The bigger question is what kind of humanitarian partner will the U.S. government be in the future?"

data.title: Grades firm slightly after whipsaw tariff-focused week
data.description:
U.S. cash crude grades firmed marginally on Friday, dealers said, steadying after a rollercoaster week of uncertainty in the market around U.S. tariffs on Mexico and Canada.

U.S. President Donald Trump imposed a 25% tariff on imports from Mexico and Canada, with an exception of a 10% levy on Canadian oil, which took effect overnight on Tuesday.

Then on Thursday Trump suspended the tariffs on most goods from Canada and Mexico, in what was the latest twist in a fluctuating trade policy that has whipsawed markets and fanned worries about inflation and growth.

Earlier this week, Mars Sour (WTC-MRS) had surged in one of the first indications that Trump's tariffs were disrupting the global oil trade.

The coastal grade, which is an attractive alternative to Mexican grade Isthmus, firmed 5 cents on Friday at a midpoint of a $1.65 premium after easing since Tuesday.

Light Louisiana Sweet (WTC-LLS), which had also eased throughout this week after being bid up on Tuesday, also firmed slightly on Friday, to a midpoint of a $3.88 premium.

Amid tariff news, dealers also will need to focus on Russia supply, after Trump said in a post on Truth Social that he was "strongly considering" sanctions on Russian banks and tariffs on Russian products because its armed forces continue attacks in Ukraine.

An indication of future oil production, U.S. energy firms this week cut the number of oil and natural gas rigs operating for the first time in six weeks, energy services firm Baker Hughes said on Friday.

* Light Louisiana Sweet (WTC-LLS) for April delivery firmed 13 cents at a midpoint of a $3.88 premium and was seen bid and offered between a $3.50 and $4.25 a barrel premium to U.S. crude futures

* Mars Sour (WTC-MRS) firmed 5 cents at a midpoint of a $1.65 premium and was seen bid and offered between a $1.55 and $1.75 a barrel premium to U.S. crude futures

* WTI Midland (WTC-WTM) firmed 5 cents at a midpoint of a $1.20 premium and was seen bid and offered between a $1.10 and $1.30 a barrel premium to U.S. crude futures

* West Texas Sour (WTC-WTS) firmed 3 cents at a midpoint of a 83-cent premium and was seen bid and offered between a 70-cent and 95-cent a barrel premium to U.S. crude futures

* WTI at East Houston (WTC-MEH), also known as MEH, traded between a $1.35 and $1.55 a barrel premium to U.S. crude futures

* ICE Brent May futures rose 90 cents to settle at $70.36 a barrel

* WTI April crude futures rose 68 cents to settle at $67.04 a barrel

* The Brent/WTI spread (WTCLc1-LCOc1) widened to last trade at minus $3.60, after hitting a high of minus $3.48 and a low of minus $3.61.

data.title: Employers Added 151,000 Jobs in February, Unemployment Rose to 4.1% — WSJ
data.description:
By Justin Lahart

The U.S. continued to generate jobs at a steady pace in February, offering reassurances that the labor market has remained relatively stable since President Trump took office. But there were warning signs that it could weaken.

The economy added a seasonally adjusted 151,000 jobs in February, the Labor Department reported Friday, slightly below the gain of 170,000 jobs economists polled by The Wall Street Journal expected to see. Still, that was better than the 125,000 jobs added in January.

The unemployment rate, which is based on a separate survey from the jobs figures, rose to 4.1% from 4%.

Economists believe that the cuts to federal government jobs under Trump came too late in the month to be captured in the February figures. But the report did show a loss of about 10,000 federal government jobs over the month, unusual both because it is a decline and because it is such a large one.

Of that, 3,500 lost jobs were at the postal service, where employment often swings higher and lower from month to month. The remaining 6,700 lost jobs represented the biggest drop in more than two years.

Morgan Stanley economists in a note Friday said they didn't believe the decline was a reflection of layoffs, "but was instead the front edge of the freeze on federal hiring." Trump put a hiring freeze in place on Jan. 20, the day he was inaugurated.

Local governments added 20,000 jobs, about half of them in education. State governments added 1,000 jobs.

Trump heralded the numbers as a success during remarks from the Oval Office and pointed to a number of investments that companies have announced, including Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. expansion plans for the U.S. He dismissed the effect of large-scale layoffs of government workers. "We had too many people in government," he said, defending a forthcoming plan to gut the Education Department.

Stocks finished the day higher, though they were still down for the week. Markets have been battered this week by Trump's back-and-forth on tariffs.

Healthcare accounted for a large portion of the February employment gain, adding 52,000 jobs. The transportation and warehousing industries added 18,000 jobs.

On the other side of the ledger, retailers shed 6,000 jobs. And the food services and drinking sector — largely restaurants and bars — lost 27,500 jobs. That could be an indication that immigration restrictions are beginning to affect the supply of workers at businesses that depend heavily on immigrant labor.

Economists expect that the combination of government job layoffs, reduced government funding, uncertainty over tariffs and immigration restrictions will at least temporarily weigh on employment growth in the months ahead. Still, some had worried more weakness would show up in the February report. Economists at High Frequency Economics, for example, had expected a gain of just 30,000 jobs.

"While we don't like being wrong, we are relieved that the February payrolls number did not print as low as we thought it might," they wrote in a note following the report's release.

Joe Brusuelas, chief economist at RSM US, said that the report showed that underlying demand for labor remained strong in February. But he cautioned that the administration's tariff moves could unsettle that in the months to come.

"This might be the last of the job reports that will not be impacted by the significant changes in trade policy that are in train," he said.

The Federal Reserve is widely expected to hold interest rates steady at its meeting in two weeks. "Many indicators show that the labor market is solid and broadly in balance," Fed Chair Jerome Powell said at a conference in New York on Friday.

Average hourly earnings rose 0.3% from January, putting them 4% above their year-earlier level.

For its jobs figure, the Labor Department surveys employers on how many people they had on their payrolls during the pay period that includes the 12th of the month; anybody who worked at all during that period in February was counted as employed.

That includes federal workers still in their probationary period after getting hired or promoted that the White House began laying off around Feb. 13. It also includes the roughly 75,000 people who accepted the White House's "deferred resignation" offer, where the White House said they don't have to work but can still get paid through the end of September.

Those layoffs will begin showing up in the jobs count in the months ahead. Exactly how many federal government workers have lost their jobs so far as a result of the "Workforce Optimization Initiative" led by Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency is unclear. Some firings might not have been announced, while in other cases fired workers have been called back to the office. There are also layoffs at private-sector employers where government contracts and grants have been canceled.

Outplacement firm Challenger, Gray & Christmas on Thursday said that it counted 63,583 layoff announcements in February in the federal workforce and in government contractors. Labor Department data also show that there has been a pickup in initial claims for unemployment benefits filed by federal government employees.

Beyond any layoffs, the government could also lose a substantial number of jobs due to attrition as a result of the hiring freeze.

Strategists at Evercore ISI worry that uncertainty over contracts and grants is having "an additional paralyzing effect on hiring at employers exposed to these funding sources or in need of regulatory approvals from agencies that are themselves paralyzed by DOGE review."

Many businesses are enthusiastic about Trump's plans to cut taxes and scale back regulations, and that could lead them to increase hiring. The White House believes that tariffs will encourage automakers and other manufacturers to bring more production, and jobs, to the U.S., and that cuts to government jobs and spending will ultimately make the economy more efficient.

Worries over tariffs could also weigh on the labor market. This is in part because uncertainty over what imports from what countries will ultimately be subject to higher tariffs, and at what rates, makes it hard for businesses to hire.

Immigration policy could also restrain the job market. The number of people coming into the U.S. began declining last summer, but since Inauguration Day appears to have dropped sharply. Additionally, worries about deportations could lead some people to withdraw from the labor force.

Write to Justin Lahart at Justin.Lahart@wsj.com

data.title: Corn, soy basis mixed amid slow selling
data.description:
Spot basis bids for soybeans and corn were mixed in the U.S. Midwest on Friday as farmers were reluctant to sell their crop amid low futures prices, while buyers kept a close eye on U.S. trade policy.

* The soy basis ticked up at an Iowa elevator and an Iowa crush plant.

* The corn basis tipped down at an Illinois processor, an Iowa river terminal and an Illinois river terminal. The corn basis rose at an Iowa elevator.

* Farmer selling has been slow as prices remain too low to encourage selling.

* Chicago Board of Trade corn futures rose for a third straight session on Friday as news of exemptions for Mexico and Canada to most U.S. tariffs allowed grain prices to stabilize after a plunge early this week.

* Soybean futures were flat to narrowly mixed.

* The ongoing saga with U.S. tariffs remained the focus of the market. On Thursday, U.S. President Donald Trump suspended the tariffs he imposed days earlier on most goods from Canada and Mexico, the latest twist in a fluctuating trade policy.

* CBOT May corn (CK25) settled 5-1/4 cents higher at $4.69-1/4 per bushel.

* May soybeans (SK25) settled down 2-1/4 cents at $10.25 per bushel.

data.title: Ohmyhome Files Preliminary Prospectus to Offer Up to $300 Million of Securities
data.description:
Ohmyhome late Friday filed a registration statement for an up to $300 million mixed-shelf offering covering ordinary shares, preferred shares, debt securities, warrants, rights and units.

The Singapore-based real estate services company said net proceeds from any future offering would be used to fund the growth of the business, working capital and other general corporate purposes. A portion of the proceeds may be used to repay debt, it said.

Ohmyhome was up almost 2% during Friday's late session.

data.title: PDF Solutions Completes secureWISE Acquisition
data.description:
PDF Solutions said Friday it completed the acquisition of secureWISE from Telit IOT Solutions Inc.

Financial details weren't disclosed, but the company said last month it agreed to acquire secureWISE for $130 million in cash, funded through a combination of cash on hand and $70 million in new bank debt.

SecureWISE is a secure, remote connectivity solution for the semiconductor manufacturing equipment industry, the company said.
